note: A strong outpatient consult — most criteria met, natural flow.
flat:
Doctor: Good morning, I'm Doctor Patel, one of the GPs here today. Please come in and have a seat, make yourself comfortable.
Patient: Thank you.
Doctor: So, tell me in your own words — what's brought you in today?
Patient: I've been getting these headaches for about three weeks, and honestly it's starting to scare me a bit.
Doctor: I'm sorry to hear that, that sounds really unsettling. Take your time and tell me more — when did they start, where do you feel them, and does anything make them better or worse?
Patient: They're at the front of my head, usually late afternoon, and worse when I've been on the computer. Paracetamol takes the edge off.
Doctor: Thank you. Can I ask what you think might be going on, or whether there's something in particular you're worried it could be?
Patient: I suppose I'm frightened it might be something serious, like a brain tumour. My aunt had one.
Doctor: That's a really understandable worry, and I'm glad you've told me — we'll work through this properly together.
Doctor: I'd like to examine you now — I'll check your blood pressure and look into the back of your eyes. I'll explain as I go, and do say if anything's uncomfortable.
Patient: Okay, that's fine.
Doctor: Everything I'm seeing is reassuring. In plain terms, this fits what we call tension-type headache — the muscles around the head tightening up, often triggered by screen time and stress. It's very common and not dangerous.
Patient: Oh, okay, that's a relief.
Doctor: We've got a couple of options. We could start with regular screen breaks and simple pain relief and review in a few weeks, or we could consider a daily preventer. What would you prefer?
Patient: I'd rather try the breaks and painkillers first.
Doctor: That sounds very sensible. Just so I know I've explained it clearly, could you tell me back what the plan is?
Patient: Take regular breaks from screens, use paracetamol when needed, and come back if it doesn't settle.
Doctor: Perfect. And importantly — if the headache suddenly becomes much worse, if you get any changes in your vision, weakness, or it wakes you from your sleep, please seek help straight away or go to A&E.
Patient: Understood, thank you.
Doctor: Before we finish, what questions do you have for me?
Patient: No, I think you've covered everything. Thanks so much.

note: A rushed consult — some competent history but no rapport, jargon, no shared plan.
flat:
Doctor: Right, what seems to be the problem?
Patient: I've had this cough for a couple of weeks and—
Doctor: Any fever? Chest pain? Coughing anything up?
Patient: Um, no fever, no blood, just a dry cough, mostly at night.
Doctor: Smoker?
Patient: I quit last year.
Doctor: Okay. It's likely a post-viral cough. It's a self-limiting condition, viral in aetiology, should resolve with conservative management.
Patient: Right... is there anything I can do?
Doctor: Honey and warm drinks. Come back if it's not gone in a month.
Patient: Okay.
Doctor: Anything else? No? Great, take care.

note: A poor consult — brusque, constant interruptions, prescribes with no rapport, info, or safety-net.
flat:
Doctor: Next. Name and date of birth?
Patient: Sarah, third of March 1990. I've been feeling really down lately and—
Doctor: How long?
Patient: A few months, I guess. It's been hard to—
Doctor: Sleeping okay? Appetite?
Patient: Not really, I—
Doctor: Right. Sounds like low mood. I'll start you on an antidepressant, take one a day.
Patient: Oh. Okay. Will it—
Doctor: Any allergies? No? Here's the prescription. Next patient, please.
